title: Global Physical Climatology
text:
Preface
This book covers global physical climatology and machine learning approaches including PINNs and Neural Operators.

title: Vector Calculus
text: Builds the differential operator vocabulary used throughout the book. Covers gradient (scalar and vector fields), the Jacobian, directional derivative, divergence, curl, and the Laplacian. Derives all operators in spherical coordinates via scale factors, and identifies the Laplace-Beltrami operator on \(S^2\). Covers line, surface, and volume integrals, and the integral theorems: Green’s, Stokes’, and the Divergence theorem.

title: 1  Differential Operators
text:
1.1 Gradient
Let \(f : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}\) be a scalar field and \(\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3\) a vector field with components \(\mathbf{F} = (F_x, F_y, F_z)\).
Definition (scalar field). The gradient of \(f : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}\) is the vector field
\[\nabla f = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x},\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial y},\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\right).\]
Intuition. \(\nabla f(\mathbf{p})\) points in the direction of steepest increase of \(f\) at \(\mathbf{p}\). Its magnitude is the rate of change in that direction. Level sets of \(f\) are everywhere perpendicular to \(\nabla f\).
Example. Let \(f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2\). Then \(\nabla f = (2x, 2y)\). At \(\mathbf{p} = (1, 1)\): \(\nabla f = (2, 2)\), pointing radially outward with magnitude \(2\sqrt{2}\).
Definition (vector field). The gradient of \(\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m\) is the \(m \times n\) tensor of partial derivatives
\[(\nabla \mathbf{F})_{ij} = \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_j}.\]
This is a second-order tensor (matrix). It maps a direction \(\hat{\mathbf{u}}\) to the directional derivative \(D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}} \mathbf{F} = (\nabla \mathbf{F})\,\hat{\mathbf{u}}\), generalizing the scalar case.
Example. Let \(\mathbf{F}(x, y) = (x^2 + y,\; xy)\). Then
\[\nabla \mathbf{F} = \begin{pmatrix} 2x & 1 \\ y & x \end{pmatrix}.\]
At \(\mathbf{p} = (1, 2)\): \(\nabla \mathbf{F} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\).
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 9.7 (scalar gradient and directional derivative); tensor gradient of vector fields is treated more fully in advanced calculus texts.

title: 1  Differential Operators
text:
1.2 Jacobian
Definition. For \(\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m\), the Jacobian matrix \(J_\mathbf{F}\) is identical to \(\nabla \mathbf{F}\):
\[J_\mathbf{F} = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial F_m}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial F_m}{\partial x_n} \end{pmatrix}.\]
The Jacobian is the best linear approximation to \(\mathbf{F}\) at a point: \(\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{h}) \approx \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{p}) + J_\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{p})\,\mathbf{h}\).
Jacobian determinant. For square \(\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n\), \(\det(J_\mathbf{F})\) measures local volume scaling under \(\mathbf{F}\). It appears in the change-of-variables formula:
\[\int_{\mathbf{F}(V)} g(\mathbf{y})\, d\mathbf{y} = \int_V g(\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}))\,|\det J_\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})|\, d\mathbf{x}.\]
Example. The map to spherical coordinates \(\mathbf{F}(r, \theta, \phi) = (r\sin\theta\cos\phi,\; r\sin\theta\sin\phi,\; r\cos\theta)\) has
\[\det(J_\mathbf{F}) = r^2 \sin\theta,\]
giving the volume element \(dV = r^2\sin\theta\, dr\, d\theta\, d\phi\).
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 9.6 (calculus review, change of variables) and Sec. 10.7 (Jacobian in the context of triple integrals).

title: 1  Differential Operators
text:
1.3 Directional Derivative
Definition. The directional derivative of \(f\) at \(\mathbf{p}\) along unit vector \(\hat{\mathbf{u}}\) is
\[D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}} f(\mathbf{p}) = \nabla f(\mathbf{p}) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{u}}.\]
This is the rate of change of \(f\) along \(\hat{\mathbf{u}}\). The gradient is the unique direction that maximizes \(D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}} f\), with maximum value \(|\nabla f(\mathbf{p})|\).
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 9.7.

title: 1  Differential Operators
text:
1.4 Divergence
Definition. The divergence of \(\mathbf{F}\) is the scalar field
\[\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}.\]
Intuition. \(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{p})\) measures the net outward flux of \(\mathbf{F}\) per unit volume at \(\mathbf{p}\). Positive divergence indicates a local source; negative indicates a sink; zero indicates incompressibility.
Example. Let \(\mathbf{F}(x, y, z) = (x, y, z)\) (radial expansion). Then
\[\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3.\]
Uniform positive divergence everywhere: every point is a source.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 9.8.

title: 1  Differential Operators
text:
1.5 Curl
Definition. The curl of \(\mathbf{F}\) is the vector field
\[\nabla \times \mathbf{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{\mathbf{x}} & \hat{\mathbf{y}} & \hat{\mathbf{z}} \\ \partial_x & \partial_y & \partial_z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix} = \left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z},\; \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x},\; \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right).\]
Intuition. \(\nabla \times \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{p})\) measures the infinitesimal rotation of \(\mathbf{F}\) at \(\mathbf{p}\). Its direction is the axis of rotation (right-hand rule); its magnitude is twice the angular velocity. A field with \(\nabla \times \mathbf{F} = \mathbf{0}\) everywhere is called irrotational.
Example. Let \(\mathbf{F}(x, y, z) = (-y, x, 0)\), solid-body rotation in the \(xy\)-plane. Then
\[\nabla \times \mathbf{F} = \left(0 - 0,\; 0 - 0,\; 1 - (-1)\right) = (0, 0, 2).\]
The rotation axis is \(+\hat{\mathbf{z}}\) with angular velocity \(1\).
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 9.9.

title: 1  Differential Operators
text:
1.6 Laplacian
Definition. The Laplacian of \(f\) is
\[
abla^2 f = 
abla \cdot (
abla f) =  rac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} +  rac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} +  rac{\partial^2 f}{\partial z^2}.\]
For a vector field, the Laplacian acts component-wise: \(
abla^2 \mathbf{F} = (
abla^2 F_x, 
abla^2 F_y, 
abla^2 F_z)\).
Intuition. \(
abla^2 f(\mathbf{p})\) is proportional to the difference between \(f(\mathbf{p})\) and the average of \(f\) over an infinitesimal sphere centered at \(\mathbf{p}\). It governs diffusion, electrostatics, and fluid flow. The equation \(
abla^2 f = 0\) (Laplace equation) characterizes fields with no local sources or sinks.
Example. Let \(f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2\). Then \(
abla^2 f = 2 - 2 = 0\): this function satisfies the Laplace equation.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 9.8 (Laplacian as div of grad); @Evans2010pde, Ch. 2 (Laplace equation and its properties).

title: 2  Integrals
text:
2.1 Line Integral
Let \(C\) be a smooth curve parametrized by \(\mathbf{r}(t)\), \(t \in [a, b]\).
Scalar form:
\[\int_C f \, ds = \int_a^b f(\mathbf{r}(t))\, |\mathbf{r}'(t)|\, dt.\]
Vector form (work):
\[\int_C \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{r} = \int_a^b \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{r}(t)) \cdot \mathbf{r}'(t)\, dt.\]
Example. Let \(\mathbf{F} = (y, 0)\) and \(C\) the segment from \((0,0)\) to \((1,1)\) parametrized as \(\mathbf{r}(t) = (t, t)\), \(t \in [0,1]\). Then \(\mathbf{r}'(t) = (1,1)\) and \(\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{r}(t)) = (t, 0)\), so
\[\int_C \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{r} = \int_0^1 t \, dt = \frac{1}{2}.\]
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 10.1.

title: 2  Integrals
text:
2.2 Surface Integral
Let \(S\) be an oriented surface with unit normal \(\hat{\mathbf{n}}\) and area element \(dS\).
Scalar form:
\[\iint_S f \, dS.\]
Flux form:
\[\iint_S \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \iint_S \mathbf{F} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \, dS.\]
Example. Let \(\mathbf{F} = (0, 0, 1)\) and \(S\) the unit disk in the \(xy\)-plane with \(\hat{\mathbf{n}} = (0, 0, 1)\). Then \(\mathbf{F} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} = 1\) everywhere, so the flux is
\[\iint_S dS = \pi.\]
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Secs. 10.5-10.6.

title: 2  Integrals
text:
2.3 Volume Integral
\[\iiint_V f \, dV = \iiint_V f(x, y, z)\, dx\, dy\, dz.\]
Example. \(f(x,y,z) = z\) over the unit cube \([0,1]^3\):
\[\iiint_{[0,1]^3} z \, dV = \int_0^1 z \, dz = \frac{1}{2}.\]
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 10.7.

title: 3  Change of Variables
text:
3.1 General Theorem
When an integral is computed over a domain that is not naturally aligned with Cartesian coordinates, a change of variables can simplify the computation. The Jacobian determinant (defined in the operators section) is the key quantity that accounts for the local volume distortion introduced by the coordinate map.
Let \(\mathbf{G} : U \to V\) be a smooth, invertible map between open sets \(U, V \subset \mathbb{R}^n\), with \(\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{u})\). Then
\[\int_V f(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} = \int_U f(\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{u}))\, |\det J_\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{u})|\, d\mathbf{u},\]
where \(J_\mathbf{G}\) is the Jacobian of \(\mathbf{G}\). The factor \(|\det J_\mathbf{G}|\) converts the volume element \(d\mathbf{u}\) in the new coordinates to the corresponding volume element \(d\mathbf{x}\) in the original coordinates.
1D case. For \(x = g(u)\) with \(g\) monotone:
\[\int_a^b f(x)\, dx = \int_{g^{-1}(a)}^{g^{-1}(b)} f(g(u))\, |g'(u)|\, du.\]
The factor \(|g'(u)|\) is the 1D Jacobian determinant.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 10.7 (change of variables in double and triple integrals).

title: 3  Change of Variables
text:
3.2 Polar Coordinates in 2D
The polar coordinate map is \(\mathbf{G}(r, \theta) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)\), so
\[J_\mathbf{G} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & r\cos\theta \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \det J_\mathbf{G} = r\cos^2\theta + r\sin^2\theta = r.\]
The area element transforms as \(dA = r\, dr\, d\theta\).
Example. Compute \(I = \iint_D e^{-(x^2 + y^2)}\, dA\) over the unit disk \(D = \{x^2 + y^2 \leq 1\}\).
In polar coordinates \(x^2 + y^2 = r^2\) and the disk becomes \(r \in [0,1]\), \(\theta \in [0, 2\pi)\):
\[I = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^1 e^{-r^2}\, r\, dr\, d\theta = 2\pi \int_0^1 r e^{-r^2}\, dr = 2\pi \left[-\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-r^2}\right]_0^1 = \pi(1 - e^{-1}).\]

title: 3  Change of Variables
text:
3.3 Spherical Coordinates in 3D
As derived in the spherical coordinates section, the Jacobian determinant of the spherical coordinate map \((r, \theta, \phi) \mapsto (x, y, z)\) is \(\det J = r^2\sin\theta\), giving the volume element
\[dV = r^2\sin\theta\, dr\, d\theta\, d\phi.\]
Example 1: Volume of a ball. Let \(B_R = \{|\mathbf{x}| \leq R\}\). Then
\[\text{Vol}(B_R) = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi}\int_0^R r^2\sin\theta\, dr\, d\theta\, d\phi = 2\pi \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{R^3}{3} = \frac{4\pi R^3}{3}.\]
Example 2: Surface area of the unit sphere. Setting \(r = 1\) constant, the area element on \(S^2\) is \(d\Omega = \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi\):
\[\text{Area}(S^2) = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi = 2\pi \cdot 2 = 4\pi.\]
Example 3: Gaussian integral in 3D. Compute \(I = \iiint_{\mathbb{R}^3} e^{-r^2}\, dV\):
\[I = \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \sin\theta\, d\theta \int_0^{\infty} e^{-r^2} r^2\, dr = 4\pi \int_0^{\infty} r^2 e^{-r^2}\, dr.\]
Using \(\int_0^\infty r^2 e^{-r^2}\, dr = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{4}\):
\[I = 4\pi \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{4} = \pi^{3/2}.\]
This equals \(\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\, dx\right)^3 = (\sqrt{\pi})^3\), confirming consistency with the 1D Gaussian integral.

title: 4  Integral Theorems
text:
4.1 Green’s Theorem
Let \(D \subset \mathbb{R}^2\) be a bounded region with piecewise smooth positively oriented boundary \(\partial D\). For \(P, Q \in C^1(\bar{D})\):
\[\oint_{\partial D} \bigl(P\, dx + Q\, dy\bigr) = \iint_D \left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial y}\right) dA.\]
With \(\mathbf{F} = (P, Q, 0)\), the right side is \(\iint_D (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{z}} \, dA\). Green’s theorem is the two-dimensional special case of Stokes’ theorem.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 10.4.

title: 4  Integral Theorems
text:
4.2 Stokes’ Theorem
Let \(S \subset \mathbb{R}^3\) be an oriented surface with piecewise smooth boundary \(\partial S\), oriented by the right-hand rule relative to \(\hat{\mathbf{n}}\). For \(\mathbf{F} \in C^1(S)\):
\[\oint_{\partial S} \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{r} = \iint_S (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot d\mathbf{S}.\]
The circulation of \(\mathbf{F}\) around the boundary equals the flux of its curl through the surface.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 10.9.

title: 4  Integral Theorems
text:
4.3 Divergence Theorem
Let \(V \subset \mathbb{R}^3\) be a bounded region with piecewise smooth outward-oriented boundary \(\partial V\). For \(\mathbf{F} \in C^1(\bar{V})\):
\[\iint_{\partial V} \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \iiint_V \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} \, dV.\]
The total outward flux through the boundary equals the integral of the divergence over the volume. This theorem is central to the derivation of Green’s identities, which underpin Green’s functions in the PDEs chapter.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 10.7; @Evans2010pde, Appendix C.2 (Green’s identities as a direct corollary).

title: 5  Spherical Coordinates
text:
5.1 Coordinate System
Spherical coordinates \((r, \theta, \phi)\) relate to Cartesian coordinates by
\[x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta,\]
where \(r \geq 0\) is the radial distance, \(\theta \in [0, \pi]\) is the polar angle (colatitude, from the north pole), and \(\phi \in [0, 2\pi)\) is the azimuthal angle (longitude). The orthonormal basis \((\hat{r}, \hat{\theta}, \hat{\phi})\) points in the directions of increasing \(r\), \(\theta\), \(\phi\) respectively, and rotates with position.

title: 5  Spherical Coordinates
text:
5.2 Scale Factors
For an orthogonal curvilinear coordinate system \((q_1, q_2, q_3)\), the scale factors are
\[h_i = \left|\frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial q_i}\right|,\]
converting coordinate differentials to physical arc lengths: \(ds_i = h_i\, dq_i\). The volume element is \(dV = h_1 h_2 h_3\, dq_1\, dq_2\, dq_3\).
For spherical coordinates, computing \(\partial \mathbf{r}/\partial r\), \(\partial \mathbf{r}/\partial \theta\), \(\partial \mathbf{r}/\partial \phi\) and taking magnitudes gives
\[h_r = 1, \qquad h_\theta = r, \qquad h_\phi = r\sin\theta,\]
and hence \(dV = r^2\sin\theta\, dr\, d\theta\, d\phi\), consistent with the Jacobian determinant from the operators section.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 10.7 (spherical volume element in the context of triple integrals).

title: 5  Spherical Coordinates
section: 5.3 General Operator Formulas for Orthogonal Curvilinear Coordinates
text:
5.3 General Operator Formulas for Orthogonal Curvilinear Coordinates
For coordinates \((q_1, q_2, q_3)\) with scale factors \((h_1, h_2, h_3)\) and vector field \(\mathbf{F} = F_1\hat{e}_1 + F_2\hat{e}_2 + F_3\hat{e}_3\):
Gradient: \[\nabla f = \frac{1}{h_1}\frac{\partial f}{\partial q_1}\hat{e}_1 + \frac{1}{h_2}\frac{\partial f}{\partial q_2}\hat{e}_2 + \frac{1}{h_3}\frac{\partial f}{\partial q_3}\hat{e}_3.\]
Divergence: \[\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3}\left[\frac{\partial(h_2 h_3 F_1)}{\partial q_1} + \frac{\partial(h_1 h_3 F_2)}{\partial q_2} + \frac{\partial(h_1 h_2 F_3)}{\partial q_3}\right].\]
Curl: \[\nabla \times \mathbf{F} = \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3} \begin{vmatrix} h_1\hat{e}_1 & h_2\hat{e}_2 & h_3\hat{e}_3 \\ \partial_{q_1} & \partial_{q_2} & \partial_{q_3} \\ h_1 F_1 & h_2 F_2 & h_3 F_3 \end{vmatrix}.\]
Laplacian: \[\nabla^2 f = \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3}\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial q_1}\!\left(\frac{h_2 h_3}{h_1}\frac{\partial f}{\partial q_1}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial q_2}\!\left(\frac{h_1 h_3}{h_2}\frac{\partial f}{\partial q_2}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial q_3}\!\left(\frac{h_1 h_2}{h_3}\frac{\partial f}{\partial q_3}\right)\right].\]
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 9.7-9.9 (operators in Cartesian); the general curvilinear derivation via scale factors is covered more fully in @Taylor2005mechanics, Ch. 16 (curvilinear coordinates).

title: 5  Spherical Coordinates
text:
5.4 Operators in Spherical Coordinates
Substituting \((h_r, h_\theta, h_\phi) = (1, r, r\sin\theta)\):
Gradient: \[\nabla f = \frac{\partial f}{\partial r}\hat{r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta}\hat{\theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial f}{\partial \phi}\hat{\phi}.\]
Divergence: \[\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial(r^2 F_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial(\sin\theta\, F_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi}.\]
Curl: \[\nabla \times \mathbf{F} = \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\!\left(\frac{\partial(\sin\theta\, F_\phi)}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial F_\theta}{\partial \phi}\right)\hat{r} + \frac{1}{r}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial(r F_\phi)}{\partial r}\right)\hat{\theta} + \frac{1}{r}\!\left(\frac{\partial(r F_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \theta}\right)\hat{\phi}.\]
Laplacian: \[\nabla^2 f = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2\frac{\partial f}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2\sin^2\theta}\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial \phi^2}.\]

title: 5  Spherical Coordinates
text:
5.5 Laplace-Beltrami Operator on \(S^2\)
On the unit sphere (\(r = 1\), constant), only the angular part of the Laplacian survives. The Laplace-Beltrami operator is
\[\Delta_{S^2} f = \frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{\sin^2\theta}\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial \phi^2}.\]
The full Laplacian decomposes as
\[\nabla^2 f = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2\frac{\partial f}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\Delta_{S^2} f.\]
\(\Delta_{S^2}\) is the operator whose eigenfunctions are the spherical harmonics, and whose Green’s function the GSNO is designed to approximate.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 12.10 (Laplace’s equation in spherical coordinates and the derivation of the spherical Laplacian); @Evans2010pde, Appendix C (notation and operator identities).

title: 5  Spherical Coordinates
text:
5.6 Worked Examples
Gradient of a radial function. Compute \(\nabla(r^2)\) in spherical coordinates.
\(f = r^2\) depends only on \(r\), so \(\partial f/\partial\theta = \partial f/\partial\phi = 0\):
\[\nabla(r^2) = \frac{\partial(r^2)}{\partial r}\hat{r} = 2r\hat{r}.\]
Consistent with the Cartesian result \(\nabla(x^2+y^2+z^2) = 2(x,y,z)\).

Divergence of a radial vector field. Let \(\mathbf{F} = r^2\hat{r}\). Compute \(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}\).
Only the radial term contributes:
\[\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial(r^2 \cdot r^2)}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial r^4}{\partial r} = \frac{4r^3}{r^2} = 4r.\]

Fundamental solution of Laplace’s equation. Show \(\nabla^2(1/r) = 0\) for \(r \neq 0\).
\[\nabla^2\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\right) = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2 \frac{d}{dr}\frac{1}{r}\right) = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(-1\right) = 0.\]
\(1/r\) is the fundamental solution of the Laplace equation in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) - the Green’s function for \(-\nabla^2\). Its angular average over any sphere centered at the origin is \(1/r\), constant on each sphere, which is why it is rotationally symmetric and satisfies \(\Delta_{S^2}(1/r) = 0\) trivially (no angular dependence).

Solid angle integral. Compute \(\int_{S^2} \cos^2\theta\, d\Omega\).
\[\int_{S^2} \cos^2\theta\, d\Omega = \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \cos^2\theta\sin\theta\, d\theta = 2\pi \int_{-1}^{1} u^2\, du = 2\pi \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{4\pi}{3}.\]
The mean value of \(\cos^2\theta\) over \(S^2\) is \(\frac{1}{3}\), consistent with isotropy: \(\langle x^2\rangle = \langle y^2\rangle = \langle z^2\rangle = \langle r^2\rangle / 3\).

title: Harmonic Analysis
text:
Develops Fourier analysis and its generalization to spherical harmonics. Fourier series and transforms introduce the idea of decomposing functions into orthogonal bases - a tool that reappears in the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), in spectral numerical methods, and in the solution of PDEs on flat domains. Spherical harmonics extend this to the sphere \(S^2\), forming the natural basis for functions on a globe. They appear again as eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator in the PDEs chapter, as the spectral basis in the GFD chapter, and as the structural foundation of the GSNO. This chapter is built once so every later chapter can use it without interruption.
Sections:

Fourier Series: orthogonal function bases, Fourier coefficients, Parseval’s theorem, convergence.
Fourier Transform: continuous transform, key properties (shift, derivative, convolution theorem), DFT and FFT.
Spherical Harmonics: eigenfunctions of \(\Delta_{S^2}\), orthonormality, spherical harmonic transform, addition theorem, convolution on \(S^2\).

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
6.1 Vector Spaces
Before Fourier series or spherical harmonics can be stated precisely, we need the ambient space in which they live and the notion of convergence they satisfy. This section develops that theory abstractly. Fourier series and spherical harmonics are then special cases of a single general structure: an orthonormal basis for a Hilbert space.
Definition. A vector space over \(\mathbb{R}\) (or \(\mathbb{C}\)) is a set \(V\) equipped with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying the usual axioms (commutativity, associativity, distributivity, identity, inverses).
The central examples here are spaces of functions:
Unlike \(\mathbb{R}^n\), these spaces are infinite-dimensional: no finite set of functions spans all of them.

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
\(C([a,b])\): continuous functions on \([a,b]\), with pointwise operations.
\(C^k([a,b])\): \(k\)-times continuously differentiable functions.
\(\mathcal{P}_n\): polynomials of degree at most \(n\).
\(L^2([a,b])\): square-integrable functions (defined below).

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
6.2 Norms and \(L^p\) Spaces
Definition. A norm on a vector space \(V\) is a function \(\|\cdot\| : V \to [0,\infty)\) satisfying:

\(\|f\| = 0\) iff \(f = 0\).
\(\|\alpha f\| = |\alpha|\|f\|\).
\(\|f + g\| \leq \|f\| + \|g\|\) (triangle inequality).

A normed space is a vector space equipped with a norm.
Definition. For \(1 \leq p < \infty\), the \(L^p\) space on a domain \(\Omega\) is
\[L^p(\Omega) = \left\{ f : \Omega \to \mathbb{C} \;\Big|\; \int_\Omega |f(x)|^p\, dx < \infty \right\},\]
with norm \(\|f\|_p = \left(\int_\Omega |f|^p\, dx\right)^{1/p}\). Functions equal almost everywhere are identified.
The case \(p = 2\) is by far the most important for harmonic analysis:
\[L^2(\Omega) = \left\{ f : \Omega \to \mathbb{C} \;\Big|\; \int_\Omega |f(x)|^2\, dx < \infty \right\}, \quad \|f\|_2 = \left(\int_\Omega |f|^2\right)^{1/2}.\]
\(L^2\) is the natural space for energy: \(\|f\|_2^2\) is the total “energy” of \(f\).
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.5 (function spaces in the context of Sturm-Liouville); @Evans2010pde, Appendix E (\(L^p\) spaces).

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
6.3 Inner Product Spaces
Definition. An inner product on a vector space \(V\) is a map \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{C}\) satisfying:

\(\langle f, f\rangle \geq 0\), with \(\langle f,f\rangle = 0\) iff \(f = 0\).
\(\langle f, g\rangle = \overline{\langle g, f\rangle}\) (conjugate symmetry).
\(\langle \alpha f + \beta g, h\rangle = \alpha\langle f,h\rangle + \beta\langle g,h\rangle\) (linearity in first argument).

The inner product induces a norm: \(\|f\| = \sqrt{\langle f,f\rangle}\).
On \(L^2(\Omega)\):
\[\langle f, g\rangle = \int_\Omega f(x)\,\overline{g(x)}\, dx.\]
Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. For any \(f, g \in V\):
\[|\langle f, g\rangle| \leq \|f\|\,\|g\|.\]
Equality holds iff \(f\) and \(g\) are proportional. This is the generalization of the dot product inequality \(|\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}| \leq |\mathbf{u}||\mathbf{v}|\) to infinite dimensions.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.5.

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
6.4 Hilbert Spaces
A normed space is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges to an element of the space. Completeness is the key property that allows infinite series to be controlled.
Definition. A Hilbert space is a complete inner product space.
Key example. \(L^2(\Omega)\) is a Hilbert space. This is not obvious: it requires showing that limits of \(L^2\)-Cauchy sequences are again in \(L^2\). The pointwise limit of a sequence of \(L^2\) functions need not be the \(L^2\) limit.
\(C([a,b])\) with the \(L^2\) norm is an inner product space but is not complete (the limit of a Cauchy sequence of continuous functions can be discontinuous). Completing it gives \(L^2([a,b])\).
Intuition. A Hilbert space is an infinite-dimensional generalization of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) with dot product. Virtually all geometric intuitions from \(\mathbb{R}^n\) carry over: projections, angles, decompositions into orthogonal components.
Further reading: @Evans2010pde, Appendix E; @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.6.

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
6.5 Orthogonality
Definition. \(f, g \in V\) are orthogonal if \(\langle f, g\rangle = 0\), written \(f \perp g\). A set \(\{e_n\}\) is:

Orthogonal if \(\langle e_n, e_m\rangle = 0\) for \(n \neq m\).
Orthonormal if additionally \(\langle e_n, e_n\rangle = 1\) for all \(n\).

Pythagorean theorem. If \(f \perp g\), then \(\|f + g\|^2 = \|f\|^2 + \|g\|^2\).
More generally, for a finite orthonormal set \(\{e_1, \ldots, e_N\}\) and coefficients \(c_n = \langle f, e_n\rangle\):
\[\left\|f - \sum_{n=1}^N c_n e_n\right\|^2 = \|f\|^2 - \sum_{n=1}^N |c_n|^2.\]
The partial sum \(\sum c_n e_n\) is the orthogonal projection of \(f\) onto \(\text{span}\{e_1,\ldots,e_N\}\), and is the best approximation to \(f\) in that subspace.
Gram-Schmidt process. Any countable linearly independent set \(\{f_n\}\) can be orthonormalized: set \(e_1 = f_1/\|f_1\|\), then iteratively subtract the projection onto the span of previous \(e_k\) and normalize.

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
6.6 Orthonormal Bases and Fourier Coefficients
Definition. An orthonormal set \(\{e_n\}_{n=1}^\infty\) in a Hilbert space \(H\) is an orthonormal basis (or complete orthonormal system) if the only element orthogonal to every \(e_n\) is zero:
\[\langle f, e_n\rangle = 0 \text{ for all } n \implies f = 0.\]
Equivalently, \(\{e_n\}\) is an ONB iff every \(f \in H\) expands as
\[f = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \langle f, e_n\rangle\, e_n,\]
where the sum converges in the norm of \(H\) (not necessarily pointwise). The coefficients \(\hat{f}_n = \langle f, e_n\rangle\) are the generalized Fourier coefficients of \(f\) with respect to \(\{e_n\}\).
Bessel’s inequality. For any ONS (not necessarily a basis) and any \(f \in H\):
\[\sum_n |\langle f, e_n\rangle|^2 \leq \|f\|^2.\]
Parseval’s identity. If \(\{e_n\}\) is an ONB:
\[\sum_n |\langle f, e_n\rangle|^2 = \|f\|^2.\]
Parseval says the ONB is “complete”: no energy is lost in the decomposition. It is equivalent to the ONB condition.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.6 (generalized Fourier series, Parseval); @Evans2010pde, Appendix E.

title: 6  Function Spaces
text:
6.7 Sturm-Liouville Theory
Orthogonal function families do not arise by accident. They are the eigenfunctions of self-adjoint differential operators - a general principle captured by Sturm-Liouville theory.
The Sturm-Liouville problem. On \([a,b]\), with weight function \(w(x) > 0\):
\[-\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(p(x)\frac{df}{dx}\right) + q(x)f = \lambda\, w(x)\, f, \qquad \text{(with boundary conditions)}.\]
Theorem. Under mild regularity conditions, the Sturm-Liouville problem has:

A countable set of real eigenvalues \(\lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2 \leq \cdots \to \infty\).
Eigenfunctions \(\{f_n\}\) that are orthogonal in the weighted \(L^2\) space with inner product \(\langle f, g\rangle_w = \int_a^b f(x)\overline{g(x)}\, w(x)\, dx\).
The eigenfunctions form a complete orthonormal basis for \(L^2_w([a,b])\).

Connection to specific bases:








Problem
Operator
Eigenfunctions




\(-f'' = \lambda f\) on \([-\pi,\pi]\), periodic BC
\(-\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\)
\(e^{inx}\), Fourier basis


Legendre’s equation on \([-1,1]\)
specific \(p,q,w\)
Legendre polynomials \(P_l\)


\(-\Delta_{S^2} f = \lambda f\) on \(S^2\)
\(-\Delta_{S^2}\)
Spherical harmonics \(Y_l^m\)



All three families are instances of the same theorem. The Fourier series and spherical harmonic expansion are both eigenfunction expansions of self-adjoint operators in Hilbert spaces.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.5 (Sturm-Liouville problems and orthogonal functions) and Sec. 12.10 (spherical harmonics as the Sturm-Liouville problem on \(S^2\)).

title: 7  Fourier Series
text:
7.1 Definition and Coefficients
The Fourier series is the concrete realization of the abstract ONB expansion from the function spaces section, applied to \(L^2([-\pi,\pi])\). The basis is the family of complex exponentials \(e^{inx}\), which are the eigenfunctions of \(-d^2/dx^2\) with periodic boundary conditions - a Sturm-Liouville problem on \([-\pi,\pi]\).
The functions \(\phi_n(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{inx}\), \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\), form an orthonormal basis for \(L^2([-\pi,\pi])\) under the inner product \(\langle f,g\rangle = \int_{-\pi}^\pi f\bar{g}\, dx\).
Definition. The Fourier series of \(f \in L^2([-\pi, \pi])\) is
\[f(x) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n\, e^{inx}, \qquad c_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\, e^{-inx}\, dx.\]
The \(c_n = \langle f, \phi_n\rangle\) are the generalized Fourier coefficients from the abstract theory. The series converges in \(L^2\); Parseval’s identity gives \(\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi |f|^2\,dx = \sum_n |c_n|^2\).
Real form. For real \(f\), writing \(e^{inx} = \cos(nx) + i\sin(nx)\):
\[f(x) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \bigl(a_n \cos(nx) + b_n \sin(nx)\bigr),\]
where \(a_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\cos(nx)\, dx\) and \(b_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\sin(nx)\, dx\).
Example. Let \(f(x) = x\) on \([-\pi, \pi]\). Then \(a_n = 0\) (odd function) and \(b_n = \frac{2(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\):
\[x = 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\sin(nx) = 2\left(\sin x - \frac{\sin 2x}{2} + \frac{\sin 3x}{3} - \cdots\right).\]
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Secs. 11.1-11.2; @BruntonKutz2022, Sec. 2.1.

title: 7  Fourier Series
text:
7.2 Convergence
For \(f \in L^2\), the Fourier series converges in mean square but need not converge pointwise everywhere. Key results:

If \(f\) is piecewise smooth, the series converges to \(f(x)\) at continuity points and to \(\frac{1}{2}[f(x^+) + f(x^-)]\) at jump discontinuities (Gibbs phenomenon near jumps).
If \(f \in C^k\), then \(|c_n| = O(n^{-k})\): smoother functions have faster-decaying coefficients.

Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.1 (convergence discussion).

title: 7  Fourier Series
text:
7.3 Worked Example: Square Wave
Let \(f\) be the \(2\pi\)-periodic square wave:
\[f(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & 0 < x < \pi \\ -1 & -\pi < x < 0 \end{cases}.\]
Since \(f\) is odd, all cosine coefficients vanish: \(a_n = 0\). For the sine coefficients:
\[b_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\sin(nx)\, dx = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi}\sin(nx)\, dx = \frac{2}{\pi}\left[-\frac{\cos(nx)}{n}\right]_0^{\pi} = \frac{2}{n\pi}(1 - \cos(n\pi)).\]
Since \(\cos(n\pi) = (-1)^n\):
\[b_n = \frac{2}{n\pi}(1 - (-1)^n) = \begin{cases} 4/(n\pi) & n \text{ odd} \\ 0 & n \text{ even} \end{cases}.\]
The Fourier series is
\[f(x) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\sin((2k+1)x)}{2k+1} = \frac{4}{\pi}\left(\sin x + \frac{\sin 3x}{3} + \frac{\sin 5x}{5} + \cdots\right).\]
The coefficients decay as \(O(1/n)\), consistent with \(f \in C^0\) but \(f \notin C^1\) (jump discontinuities). Near the jumps, the partial sums overshoot by approximately \(18\%\) regardless of how many terms are included (Gibbs phenomenon).

title: 8  Fourier Transform
text:
8.1 Definition
The Fourier transform extends Fourier series to functions on \(\mathbb{R}\), replacing the discrete sum over frequencies with a continuous integral.
Definition. For \(f \in L^1(\mathbb{R})\), the Fourier transform is
\[\hat{f}(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, e^{-2\pi i \xi x}\, dx.\]
The inverse Fourier transform recovers \(f\) from \(\hat{f}\):
\[f(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{f}(\xi)\, e^{2\pi i \xi x}\, d\xi.\]
\(\hat{f}(\xi)\) is the amplitude of the frequency component \(\xi\) in \(f\). The transform is well-defined and invertible for \(f \in L^2(\mathbb{R})\) (Plancherel theorem).
Alternative convention. With angular frequency \(\omega = 2\pi\xi\):
\[\hat{f}(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, e^{-i\omega x}\, dx, \qquad f(x) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{f}(\omega)\, e^{i\omega x}\, d\omega.\]
Both conventions are standard; the choice affects normalization factors.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.7 (Fourier integral) and Sec. 11.9 (Fourier transform); @BruntonKutz2022, Sec. 2.1.

title: 8  Fourier Transform
text:
8.2 Key Properties
Let \(\mathcal{F}[f] = \hat{f}\).
Linearity: \(\mathcal{F}[\alpha f + \beta g] = \alpha\hat{f} + \beta\hat{g}\).
Shift: \(\mathcal{F}[f(x - a)](\xi) = e^{-2\pi i \xi a}\hat{f}(\xi)\). Shifting in space is multiplication by a phase in frequency.
Derivative: \(\mathcal{F}[f^{(n)}](\xi) = (2\pi i \xi)^n \hat{f}(\xi)\). Differentiation in space is multiplication by \(i\xi\) in frequency. This is the key property that converts differential equations into algebraic equations.
Convolution theorem: For \((f * g)(x) = \int f(y)\, g(x-y)\, dy\):
\[\mathcal{F}[f * g] = \hat{f} \cdot \hat{g}.\]
Convolution in the spatial domain is pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain.
Parseval / Plancherel:
\[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |f(x)|^2\, dx = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |\hat{f}(\xi)|^2\, d\xi.\]
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.9 (properties and convolution theorem).

title: 8  Fourier Transform
text:
8.3 Differential Equations via Fourier Transform
The derivative property makes the Fourier transform a natural tool for solving linear PDEs with constant coefficients. Applying \(\mathcal{F}\) converts \(\partial_x \to 2\pi i\xi\), turning a PDE into an algebraic equation in frequency space.
Example. Solve \(f'(x) + af(x) = g(x)\) on \(\mathbb{R}\). Taking the Fourier transform:
\[(2\pi i\xi + a)\hat{f}(\xi) = \hat{g}(\xi) \implies \hat{f}(\xi) = \frac{\hat{g}(\xi)}{2\pi i\xi + a}.\]
Inverting gives \(f = \mathcal{F}^{-1}[\hat{g}/(2\pi i\xi + a)]\). This solution is a convolution \(f = g * h\) where \(h = \mathcal{F}^{-1}[1/(2\pi i\xi + a)]\) is the impulse response - the Green’s function of the operator \(\partial_x + a\).
Further reading: @Evans2010pde, Ch. 4 (Fourier transform applied to PDEs, Green’s functions on \(\mathbb{R}^n\)).

title: 8  Fourier Transform
text:
8.4 Discrete Fourier Transform
For a finite sequence \(x_0, \ldots, x_{N-1}\), the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) is
\[X_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_n\, e^{-2\pi i kn/N}, \qquad k = 0, \ldots, N-1.\]
The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) computes the DFT in \(O(N\log N)\) rather than \(O(N^2)\). The convolution theorem holds exactly for the DFT, making the FFT the computational backbone of spectral methods and the Fourier Neural Operator.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 11.9 (DFT and FFT); @BruntonKutz2022, Sec. 2.2 (FFT and applications in data-driven methods).

title: 8  Fourier Transform
text:
8.5 Key Identities: Derivations
All derivations below use the convention \(\hat{f}(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\, dx\).

8.5.1 Derivative Property
Claim. \(\mathcal{F}[f^{(n)}](\xi) = (2\pi i\xi)^n \hat{f}(\xi)\).
Derivation (n=1). Integrate by parts, assuming \(f(x) \to 0\) as \(|x| \to \infty\):
\[\mathcal{F}[f'](\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f'(x)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\, dx = \underbrace{\left[f(x) e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\right]_{-\infty}^{\infty}}_{= 0} + 2\pi i\xi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\, dx = 2\pi i\xi\, \hat{f}(\xi).\]
Applying the same identity \(n\) times gives the general result. Differentiation in the spatial domain is multiplication by \(2\pi i\xi\) in the frequency domain, converting a differential operator into an algebraic one.



8.5.2 Convolution Theorem
Claim. \(\mathcal{F}[f * g](\xi) = \hat{f}(\xi)\cdot\hat{g}(\xi)\).
Derivation. Write out the transform of the convolution and swap the order of integration (Fubini):
\[\mathcal{F}[f * g](\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(y)\, g(x-y)\, dy\right) e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\, dx = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(y) \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x-y)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\, dx\right) dy.\]
Substitute \(u = x - y\) in the inner integral (\(du = dx\), \(x = u + y\)):
\[= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(y) \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(u)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi(u+y)}\, du\right) dy = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(y)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi y}\, dy \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(u)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi u}\, du = \hat{f}(\xi)\cdot\hat{g}(\xi).\]
The reverse direction also holds: pointwise multiplication in frequency corresponds to convolution in space, \(\mathcal{F}^{-1}[\hat{f}\cdot\hat{g}] = f * g\). This is the basis for the FNO: learned filters applied in the frequency domain correspond to convolution operators in the spatial domain.



8.5.3 Gaussian Identity
Claim. For \(a > 0\): \(\mathcal{F}[e^{-\pi a x^2}](\xi) = a^{-1/2}\, e^{-\pi \xi^2/a}\).
In particular, \(\mathcal{F}[e^{-\pi x^2}] = e^{-\pi\xi^2}\): the standard Gaussian is a fixed point of the Fourier transform.
Derivation. Let \(I(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\pi a x^2} e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\, dx\). Complete the square in the exponent:
\[-\pi a x^2 - 2\pi i\xi x = -\pi a\!\left(x + \frac{i\xi}{a}\right)^2 - \frac{\pi\xi^2}{a}.\]
Factoring out the \(\xi\)-dependent constant:
\[I(\xi) = e^{-\pi\xi^2/a} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\pi a(x + i\xi/a)^2}\, dx.\]
The substitution \(u = \sqrt{a}(x + i\xi/a)\) (shifting the contour in the complex plane, justified by analyticity) gives \(du = \sqrt{a}\, dx\) and:
\[I(\xi) = e^{-\pi\xi^2/a} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\pi u^2}\, du = \frac{e^{-\pi\xi^2/a}}{\sqrt{a}},\]
using the Gaussian integral \(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\pi u^2}\, du = 1\).
The Gaussian plays a central role in analysis because it is maximally concentrated simultaneously in space and frequency (saturating the uncertainty principle), and because it is self-dual under the Fourier transform.



8.5.4 Dirac Delta
The Dirac delta \(\delta(x)\) is the distribution satisfying \(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x)\, f(x)\, dx = f(0)\) for any test function \(f\).
Claim. \(\mathcal{F}[\delta](\xi) = 1\) and \(\mathcal{F}[1](\xi) = \delta(\xi)\).
Derivation of \(\mathcal{F}[\delta] = 1\).
\[\hat{\delta}(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x)\, e^{-2\pi i\xi x}\, dx = e^{-2\pi i\xi \cdot 0} = 1.\]
A point mass in space has equal energy at all frequencies - a flat spectrum. This is the distributional analogue of the impulse response: the Green’s function of a differential operator \(L\) is the function \(G\) satisfying \(LG = \delta\), and in frequency space this becomes \(\hat{L}(\xi)\hat{G}(\xi) = 1\), so \(\hat{G}(\xi) = 1/\hat{L}(\xi)\).
Shifted delta. \(\mathcal{F}[\delta(\cdot - a)](\xi) = e^{-2\pi i\xi a}\). A pure spatial shift is a linear phase in frequency.

title: 9  Spherical Harmonics
text:
9.1 Definition
The spherical harmonics are the eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator \(\Delta_{S^2}\) on \(S^2\), playing the role of \(e^{inx}\) on the sphere. They are the instance of the Sturm-Liouville framework (see the function spaces section) for the operator \(-\Delta_{S^2}\) with the standard \(L^2(S^2)\) inner product.
In spherical coordinates \((\theta, \phi)\) with \(\theta \in [0,\pi]\) (colatitude) and \(\phi \in [0, 2\pi)\) (longitude), the spherical harmonics are
\[Y_l^m(\theta, \phi) = \sqrt{\frac{(2l+1)}{4\pi}\frac{(l-m)!}{(l+m)!}}\, P_l^m(\cos\theta)\, e^{im\phi},\]
where \(l = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\) is the degree, \(m = -l, \ldots, l\) is the order, and \(P_l^m\) are the associated Legendre polynomials. There are \(2l+1\) harmonics of each degree, and infinitely many degrees.
Eigenvalue equation. The spherical harmonics satisfy
\[\Delta_{S^2} Y_l^m = -l(l+1)\, Y_l^m.\]
The eigenvalue \(-l(l+1)\) depends only on degree \(l\), not order \(m\): each eigenspace has dimension \(2l+1\).
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Secs. 12.10-12.11 (Laplace’s equation in spherical coordinates, Legendre polynomials, and associated Legendre functions).

title: 9  Spherical Harmonics
text:
9.2 Orthogonality and Completeness
The \(\{Y_l^m\}\) form an ONB for \(L^2(S^2)\) in the sense of the function spaces section. The specific orthonormality relation is
\[\int_{S^2} Y_l^m(\theta,\phi)\, \overline{Y_{l'}^{m'}(\theta,\phi)}\, d\Omega = \delta_{ll'}\delta_{mm'},\]
where \(d\Omega = \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi\). The expansion and Parseval identity follow from the general ONB theory:
\[f(\theta,\phi) = \sum_{l=0}^{\infty}\sum_{m=-l}^{l} \hat{f}_l^m\, Y_l^m(\theta,\phi), \qquad \hat{f}_l^m = \int_{S^2} f\, \overline{Y_l^m}\, d\Omega, \qquad \int_{S^2} |f|^2\, d\Omega = \sum_{l,m} |\hat{f}_l^m|^2.\]
This is the spherical harmonic transform, the analogue of the Fourier series on \(S^2\).
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 12.11 (orthogonality of Legendre polynomials, basis for the expansion).

title: 9  Spherical Harmonics
text:
9.3 Low-degree Examples

\(Y_0^0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi}}\): constant function, \(l=0\).
\(Y_1^0 = \sqrt{\frac{3}{4\pi}}\cos\theta\): proportional to \(z/r\), the north-south dipole.
\(Y_1^{\pm 1} = \mp\sqrt{\frac{3}{8\pi}}\sin\theta\, e^{\pm i\phi}\): equatorial dipoles.

Degree \(l\) harmonics oscillate \(l\) times across the sphere. High \(l\) corresponds to small-scale features; low \(l\) to large-scale structure.

title: 9  Spherical Harmonics
text:
9.4 Addition Theorem
For any two directions \(\hat{\mathbf{r}}, \hat{\mathbf{r}}' \in S^2\) with angle \(\gamma\) between them:
\[P_l(\cos\gamma) = \frac{4\pi}{2l+1}\sum_{m=-l}^{l} Y_l^m(\hat{\mathbf{r}})\, \overline{Y_l^m(\hat{\mathbf{r}}')},\]
where \(P_l\) is the Legendre polynomial of degree \(l\). This identity is used to express rotationally symmetric kernels (such as Green’s functions on \(S^2\)) in terms of spherical harmonics.
Further reading: @Kreyszig2011aem, Sec. 12.11 (Legendre polynomials and their properties).

title: 9  Spherical Harmonics
text:
9.5 Convolution on \(S^2\)
A rotationally symmetric kernel \(K(\hat{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{r}}')\) (depending only on the angle between two points) acts by convolution on the sphere. By the addition theorem, convolution with such a kernel is diagonal in the spherical harmonic basis: each coefficient \(\hat{f}_l^m\) is multiplied by a degree-dependent factor \(\hat{K}_l\). This diagonalization property underlies both spectral numerical methods and the GSNO architecture.
Further reading: @Tang2025gsno (GSNO paper, Sec. 2-3, where this diagonalization is the direct basis for the architecture); @Bonev2023sfno (Sec. 2, spherical harmonic convolution in the SFNO).

title: 9  Spherical Harmonics
text:
9.6 Worked Example: Expanding \(\cos^2\theta\)
Expand \(f(\theta, \phi) = \cos^2\theta\) in spherical harmonics.
Since \(f\) is independent of \(\phi\), only \(m = 0\) terms appear. Write \(\cos^2\theta\) in terms of Legendre polynomials using the identity \(P_2(t) = \frac{1}{2}(3t^2 - 1)\), so \(t^2 = \frac{1}{3}(2P_2(t) + 1)\):
\[\cos^2\theta = \frac{1}{3}(2P_2(\cos\theta) + P_0(\cos\theta)).\]
Converting to spherical harmonics with \(Y_l^0 = \sqrt{\frac{2l+1}{4\pi}} P_l(\cos\theta)\):
\[P_0(\cos\theta) = \sqrt{4\pi}\, Y_0^0, \qquad P_2(\cos\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{4\pi}{5}}\, Y_2^0.\]
Substituting:
\[\cos^2\theta = \frac{\sqrt{4\pi}}{3}\, Y_0^0 + \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{\frac{4\pi}{5}}\, Y_2^0.\]
Only \(l = 0\) and \(l = 2\) components are nonzero. This is expected: \(\cos^2\theta\) is a degree-2 polynomial in \(\cos\theta\), and the \(l\)-th spherical harmonic captures exactly degree-\(l\) angular structure.

title: 9  Spherical Harmonics
section: 9.7 Worked Example: Verifying the Eigenvalue Equation
text:
9.7 Worked Example: Verifying the Eigenvalue Equation
Verify \(\Delta_{S^2} Y_1^0 = -2\, Y_1^0\) directly, where \(Y_1^0(\theta,\phi) = \sqrt{3/4\pi}\cos\theta\).
Since \(Y_1^0\) is independent of \(\phi\), the \(\partial_\phi^2\) term vanishes:
\[\Delta_{S^2} Y_1^0 = \frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\sqrt{\frac{3}{4\pi}}\cos\theta\right) = \sqrt{\frac{3}{4\pi}}\frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}(-\sin^2\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{3}{4\pi}}\frac{-2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = -2\,Y_1^0.\]
This confirms \(\Delta_{S^2} Y_1^0 = -l(l+1)\, Y_1^0 = -1\cdot 2\, Y_1^0\).

title: Partial Differential Equations
text: Focuses on the Laplace and Poisson equations, and their solution via Green’s functions. The Green’s function encodes how a differential operator responds to a point source - it is the theoretical object that the GSNO is designed to learn. On the sphere, the Green’s function for the Laplace-Beltrami operator is expressed in terms of the spherical harmonics from the previous chapter. Understanding what a Green’s function is, and why it solves PDEs, is the direct prerequisite for understanding what the GSNO is doing at a conceptual level.

title: Geophysical Fluid Dynamics
text: Introduces the physical system the model will be applied to. Covers the rotating-Earth framework - the Coriolis parameter, the \(f\)- and \(\beta\)-plane approximations, and Rossby waves as the dominant large-scale atmospheric wave mode. Derives the Conservation of Potential Vorticity and the Barotropic Vorticity Equation (BVE), which is the PDE the GSNO will be trained to solve. Without this chapter the model is a black box; with it, the architecture choices and the choice of Green’s function make physical sense.

title: 10  Basic
text:
10.1 Material Derivative
Let \(\vec{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3\) denote position, \(t\) time, \(\vec{v}(\vec{x}, t)\) the velocity field, \(\rho(\vec{x}, t)\) the mass density, \(p(\vec{x}, t)\) the pressure, \(\nu\) the kinematic viscosity, and \(\vec{F}_b(\vec{x}, t)\) the body force per unit mass.
The rate of change following a fluid parcel. For scalar \(\phi(\vec{x}, t)\) and vector \(\vec{b}(\vec{x}, t)\):
\[
\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \phi
~~~,~~~
\frac{D\vec{b}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{b}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla) \vec{b}
\]
The first term is local change at a fixed point; the second is advection.

title: 10  Basic
text:
10.2 Mass Continuity
\[
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0
\]
Conservation of mass. Expanding the divergence: \(\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0\).
For incompressible flow, \(\rho\) is constant along parcel trajectories (\(D\rho/Dt = 0\)), giving the incompressibility constraint:
\[
\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0
\]
The velocity field is divergence-free: fluid parcels may deform but not change volume. This eliminates density as a prognostic variable and couples velocity to pressure through the momentum equation, where \(p\) acts as a Lagrange multiplier enforcing this constraint.

title: 10  Basic
text:
10.3 Momentum Equation
\[
\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{F}_b
\]
Start from Newton’s second law for a small parcel of fluid with volume \(\delta V\):
\[
\vec{F} = m \vec{a}
\]
The parcel has mass \(m = \rho \, \delta V\) and acceleration \(\vec{a} = D\vec{v}/Dt\), so:
\[
\vec{F} = \rho \, \delta V \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}
\]
Divide both sides by \(\delta V\) to express everything per unit volume:
\[
\frac{\vec{F}}{\delta V} = \rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}
\]
The left-hand side is the sum of force densities (force per unit volume) acting on the parcel: pressure gradient \((-\nabla p)\), viscous stress \((\mu \nabla^2 \vec{v}\), where \(\mu = \rho \nu\) is the dynamic viscosity), and body forces \((\rho \vec{F}_b)\).

title: 11  Rotating Frame
text:
11.1 Inertial to Rotating Frame
The Earth rotates with angular velocity \(\vec{\Omega}\), where \(|\vec{\Omega}| = \Omega \approx 7.292 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{rad/s}\). The equations of motion derived in the inertial (non-rotating) frame must be transformed to account for this rotation.
Let subscripts \(I\) and \(R\) denote quantities in the inertial and rotating frames. For any vector \(\vec{A}\):
\[
\left(\frac{d\vec{A}}{dt}\right)_I = \left(\frac{d\vec{A}}{dt}\right)_R + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{A}
\]
Applying this to the position vector \(\vec{r}\) gives the velocity transformation:
\[
\vec{v}_I = \vec{v}_R + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{r}
\]
Applying it again to \(\vec{v}_I\) gives the acceleration:
\[
\left(\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}\right)_I = \left(\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}\right)_R + 2\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_R + \vec{\Omega} \times (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{r})
\]

title: 11  Rotating Frame
section: 11.2 Momentum Equation in the Rotating Frame
text:
11.2 Momentum Equation in the Rotating Frame
Substituting into the momentum equation from the previous section, and dropping the \(R\) subscript (all quantities are now in the rotating frame):
\[
\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} - 2\rho \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} - \rho \vec{\Omega} \times (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{r}) + \rho \vec{g}
\]
Two new terms appear:

\(-2\rho \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}\) is the Coriolis force. It acts perpendicular to the velocity, deflecting moving parcels to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern. It does no work (it changes direction, not speed).
\(-\rho \vec{\Omega} \times (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{r})\) is the centrifugal force. It points outward from the rotation axis. In practice it is absorbed into an effective gravity \(\vec{g}^* = \vec{g} - \vec{\Omega} \times (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{r})\), and the geopotential surfaces (including “sea level”) already account for it.

With this absorption, the equation becomes:
\[
\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} - 2\rho \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}^*
\]

title: 11  Rotating Frame
text:
11.3 The Coriolis Parameter
For large-scale atmospheric and oceanic flows, the vertical component of the Coriolis force dominates. Let \(\varphi\) denote latitude. The Coriolis parameter is:
\[
f = 2\Omega \sin\varphi
\]
At the equator \(f = 0\); at the poles \(f = \pm 2\Omega\). The horizontal component of the Coriolis acceleration reduces to \(f \hat{k} \times \vec{v}_h\), where \(\vec{v}_h\) is the horizontal velocity and \(\hat{k}\) is the local vertical unit vector.

title: 11  Rotating Frame
text:
11.4 \(f\)-plane and \(\beta\)-plane
For motions whose horizontal scale \(L\) is small compared to the Earth’s radius \(a\), a local Cartesian approximation is used.
\(f\)-plane: \(f\) is treated as constant, equal to its value at a reference latitude \(\varphi_0\):
\[
f = f_0 = 2\Omega \sin\varphi_0
\]
Valid when the flow does not span a large range of latitudes. Captures inertial oscillations and geostrophic balance but not Rossby wave dynamics.
\(\beta\)-plane: \(f\) varies linearly with the northward coordinate \(y\):
\[
f = f_0 + \beta y ~~~,~~~ \beta = \frac{df}{dy} = \frac{2\Omega \cos\varphi_0}{a}
\]
The \(\beta\)-effect is the minimal ingredient needed to support Rossby waves and westward phase propagation of large-scale disturbances.

title: 12  Vorticity
text:
12.1 Definitions
Let \(\vec{v}(\vec{x}, t)\) be the velocity field. The vorticity is:
\[
\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{v}
\]
Vorticity measures local rotation of fluid elements. A flow with \(\vec{\omega} = 0\) everywhere is irrotational.
The circulation around a closed curve \(C\) is:
\[
\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{v} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{S}
\]
where \(S\) is any surface bounded by \(C\) (by Stokes’ theorem). Circulation is the integrated vorticity through the surface, or equivalently the net tangential velocity around the loop.

title: 12  Vorticity
text:
12.2 Vorticity Equation
Take the curl of the momentum equation for an inviscid barotropic fluid (\(p = p(\rho)\)):
\[
\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v} - \vec{\omega}(\nabla \cdot \vec{v})
\]
The left-hand side is advection of vorticity. On the right:

\((\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}\) is vortex stretching and tilting. Velocity gradients along the vorticity direction amplify or reorient vortex tubes.
\(-\vec{\omega}(\nabla \cdot \vec{v})\) accounts for divergence: expanding fluid dilutes vorticity.

For incompressible flow (\(\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0\)), the second term vanishes.
A baroclinic term \(\frac{1}{\rho^2} \nabla \rho \times \nabla p\) appears when density is not a function of pressure alone. It generates vorticity wherever isosurfaces of \(\rho\) and \(p\) are misaligned.

title: 12  Vorticity
text:
12.3 Kelvin’s Circulation Theorem
For an inviscid, barotropic fluid with only conservative body forces, the circulation around a material curve \(C(t)\) (a curve moving with the fluid) is conserved:
\[
\frac{D\Gamma}{Dt} = 0
\]
Vortex lines are frozen into the fluid: they stretch and deform with it but cannot be created or destroyed. This breaks down when viscosity, baroclinicity, or non-conservative forces are present.

title: 12  Vorticity
section: 12.4 Vorticity Equation in a Rotating Frame
text:
12.4 Vorticity Equation in a Rotating Frame
In a frame rotating with angular velocity \(\vec{\Omega}\), define the absolute vorticity \(\vec{\omega}_a = \vec{\omega} + 2\vec{\Omega}\). The vorticity equation becomes:
\[
\frac{D\vec{\omega}_a}{Dt} = (\vec{\omega}_a \cdot \nabla)\vec{v} - \vec{\omega}_a(\nabla \cdot \vec{v})
\]
The planetary vorticity \(2\vec{\Omega}\) enters on equal footing with the relative vorticity \(\vec{\omega}\). For large-scale geophysical flows, \(|2\vec{\Omega}| \gg |\vec{\omega}|\), so planetary rotation dominates the vorticity budget.
Restricting to the vertical component for shallow horizontal flow, with \(f = 2\Omega \sin\varphi\):
\[
\frac{D}{Dt}(\zeta + f) = -(\zeta + f)\nabla_h \cdot \vec{v}_h
\]
where \(\zeta = \hat{k} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{v})\) is the vertical component of relative vorticity and \(\nabla_h \cdot \vec{v}_h\) is the horizontal divergence.

title: 12  Vorticity
text:
12.5 Circulation Theorem and the \(\beta\)-effect
Kelvin’s theorem applied to the rotating Earth conserves absolute circulation. A fluid column moving poleward encounters increasing \(f\), so \(\zeta\) must decrease to compensate. This coupling between meridional motion and relative vorticity is the \(\beta\)-effect and is the restoring mechanism for Rossby waves.

title: 12  Vorticity
text:
12.6 Barotropic Vorticity Equation
For a two-dimensional, incompressible, inviscid flow on a \(\beta\)-plane, horizontal divergence vanishes (\(\nabla_h \cdot \vec{v}_h = 0\)). The vorticity equation reduces to:
\[
\frac{\partial \zeta}{\partial t} + J(\psi, \zeta) + \beta \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 0
\]
where \(\psi\) is the streamfunction defined by \(u = -\partial \psi / \partial y\), \(v = \partial \psi / \partial x\), and \(J(\psi, \zeta) = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\frac{\partial \zeta}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\frac{\partial \zeta}{\partial x}\) is the Jacobian. Since \(\zeta = \nabla^2 \psi\), this is a single prognostic equation for \(\psi\).
This is the Barotropic Vorticity Equation (BVE) - the PDE that the GSNO will be trained to solve.

title: Neural Operators
text: Covers the machine learning framework for learning mappings between function spaces rather than between finite-dimensional vectors. Introduces the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) as the foundational architecture - it applies learned convolutions in the Fourier domain, directly leveraging the harmonic analysis from chapter 2. Then introduces the Green’s Spherical Neural Operator (GSNO), which replaces the Fourier basis with spherical harmonics and frames the learned map as approximating a Green’s function on \(S^2\) - connecting chapters 2, 3, and 4 into a single model. This is the destination the book has been building toward.
